title: When Evaluating Groups: - Ask Yourself:
subtitle: Judge Individuals, Not Groups
description:
When evaluating groups:
- Ask yourself: "Am I judging the group based on one person’s actions?"
- Separate individuals: Focus on specific behaviors or contributions, not stereotypes.
- Avoid sweeping judgments: Base evaluations on evidence, not generalizations.

Example: If one team member underperforms, don’t assume the whole team is inefficient.

howTo.title: Pause and Reflect
howTo.summary: When you notice a sweeping judgment about a group, pause and ask yourself, 'Am I judging the group based on one person’s actions?' This helps to challenge automatic generalizations.

howTo.title: Gather Evidence
howTo.summary: Collect two pieces of evidence related to the situation. This could include counts of relevant incidents or contributions from individuals. Aim for specific data to inform your judgment.

howTo.title: Adjust Your Perspective
howTo.summary: Based on the evidence gathered, update your stance. Decide whether to maintain your initial impression or adjust it according to the data. This promotes a more nuanced understanding.

howTo.title: Implement the Habit
howTo.summary: Practice this ritual consistently in medium to high-impact evaluations. Make it a part of your decision-making process to reduce unfair generalizations and improve accuracy.

howTo.title: Check in with LifeOS Brali app
howTo.summary: Log your results in the Brali LifeOS app to track your progress and reinforce the habit. Visit https://metalhatscats.com/life-os/judge-individuals-not-groups/ to get started.
